summary: Performed static QA review of Movana Flutter app skeleton (no Flutter/Dart SDK available for runtime tests). Verified architecture, assets, routing, major screens, Riverpod state wiring, and Firebase/Cloud Functions skeleton.

critical.issue: Proxy accepts any TMDB path with server bearer token and no allowlist/rate-limit/auth guard, creating potential abuse/cost/security risk.

minor.issue: req.query is forwarded wholesale to TMDB, including internal 'path' param; should sanitize forwarded params.

integration_issues.issue: Firebase config files and project credentials are not present, so real Firebase auth/storage/firestore flows are not executable in this environment.

action_items: Harden Cloud Function tmdbProxy with endpoint allowlist, optional app/auth check, and abuse protection (rate limit / budget guard).
Sanitize proxy query before forwarding to TMDB (remove non-TMDB keys like path).
Add /app/memory/test_credentials.md for future auth QA workflow compliance.
If web support is planned, add CORS handling and origin controls for tmdbProxy.
critical_code_review_comments: No Flutter/Dart test suite present for regression (widget/service tests missing).
Auth and TMDB data flows are intentionally MOCKED/placeholder-based; production readiness depends on Firebase/TMDB secret wiring.
Current Firebase bootstrap swallows all init exceptions; consider structured logging for diagnosability in non-demo builds.
context_for_next_testing_agent: Flutter/Dart SDK unavailable in container. Static validation done: assets exist, splash 2s route logic present, login mock/guest flows wired, home/filter/list/details/watchlist/theatre/profile/admin skeletons present. functions/index.js syntax valid via node --check, firebase.json JSON valid. Prior test_reports folder was absent; initialized iteration_1.
rca of the issue: Primary high-risk issue is tmdbProxy over-permissiveness: reproduction by inspecting /app/functions/index.js shows user-controlled path is passed to tmdbGet with privileged TMDB token and no allowlist. This can be abused to hit arbitrary TMDB endpoints and increase cost/exposure. Mitigation: restrict to approved endpoint patterns, strip unknown params, add basic auth/app-check/rate-limiting, and return sanitized error responses.
